title: Outlook-at-Risk

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The New York Fed's Outlook-at-Risk is a research methodology that quantifies downside risks to real GDP growth, upside risks to unemployment, and two-sided risks to CPI inflation by estimating conditional distributions based on financial conditions (using the ECB's Composite Indicator of Systemic Stress). The tool uses quantile regressions on Blue Chip Economic Indicators forecasts and is published monthly on the third Wednesday. It is explicitly not an official Fed forecast and is a joint product of the NY Fed's Financial Stability Division and Applied Macroeconomics and Econometrics Center.
key_points: Outlook-at-Risk estimates conditional distributions for three variables: real GDP growth, unemployment rate, and CPI inflation based on financial conditions
The tool uses the Composite Indicator of Systemic Stress (CISS) from the ECB as its measure of overall U.S. financial conditions
Near-term forecasts (1-4 quarters ahead) are sourced from the Blue Chip Economic Indicators Survey (BCEI)
Realized data uses third-release real output from Philadelphia Fed Real-Time Data, U-3 unemployment from BLS, and CPI from BLS
Distributions are estimated using quantile regressions with only information available at the time to avoid look-ahead bias
The methodology, based on Adrian, Boyarchenko, and Giannone (2019), shows that tight financial conditions presage higher probability of real activity declines
Estimates are published monthly at 10 a.m. on the third Wednesday of each month, covering data back to January 1989
why_it_matters: This tool provides a systematic, quantitative framework for measuring tail risks to the macro outlook by linking financial conditions to the distribution of future economic outcomes. Market participants can use it to assess whether current financial conditions suggest elevated downside risk to growth or upside risk to unemployment and inflation. Note: This is a methodology explanation page; actual current risk estimates would be found in separate data release pages.
macro_context: The methodology demonstrates a negative relationship between financial conditions and real activity risk, with this relationship strongest when the economy is most vulnerable. For GDP growth, left-tail percentiles (10th percentile) indicate downside risk; for unemployment, right-tail percentiles (90th percentile) indicate deterioration risk; for inflation, both tails matter. The gap between 90th and 10th percentiles measures uncertainty about future outcomes.
